### **1. Living Room LED Light Output and Energy Consumption**

The natural light entering the living room is modeled by the function:

$$
N(t) = 300 + 200\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{12}t\right)
$$

This function gives the natural light level in lumens at time $ t $, where $ t $ is the number of hours after sunrise. To maintain a constant total illumination level of 500 lumens, the LED light output $ L(t) $ must adjust to compensate for the variation in natural light:

$$
L(t) = 500 - N(t) = 500 - \left(300 + 200\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{12}t\right)\right)
$$

Simplifying:

$$
L(t) = 200 - 200\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{12}t\right)
$$

This function represents the LED light output needed at time $ t $ to maintain a total illumination of 500 lumens.

---

Now, to calculate the **total energy consumption** of the LEDs over a 24-hour period, we integrate the LED output function over the 24-hour period and multiply by the power consumption rate of 0.1 watts per lumen-hour.

$$
\text{Total Energy Consumption} = \int_0^{24} L(t) \cdot 0.1 \, dt
$$

Substitute $ L(t) = 200 - 200\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{12}t\right) $:

$$
\text{Total Energy Consumption} = 0.1 \int_0^{24} \left(200 - 200\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{12}t\right)\right) dt
$$

Break the integral into two parts:

$$
= 0.1 \left[ \int_0^{24} 200 \, dt - \int_0^{24} 200\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{12}t\right) dt \right]
$$
